learning_outcomes.description: Identify the most appropriate research methods and practices to utilise for specific research contexts
Design a variety of research methods to investigate environmental, social and/or planning issues
Utilise one or more research methods to better understand environmental, social and/or planning issues
Analyse quantitative and qualitative research data using appropriate software tools
Reflect on a variety of research methods to appraise their applicability and utility, while considering your key learnings and challenges
Employ oral, written and graphic communication skills to effectively articulate and apply your knowledge of research methods and data
Practice the ethical conduct of research to ensure robust and safe research practices
Work effectively in a team to solve complex environmental, social and/or planning issues through research

To be submitted during students' scheduled workshop.
assessments.task_description:
The first part of the course (before the mid-semester break) is dedicated to learning and applying research skills. Students will need to complete five weekly applied research assessments across the semester. Guidance for these assessments will be delivered in each weekly workshop, with the task due by the end of your weekly workshop time.

Important note: Seven weekly assessments will be available, and students should submit only five of these. Students may choose which five weekly assessments they would like to submit. This is designed to support flexibility for students in this course. Each submitted weekly assessment is worth 5%.

The weekly assessments are intended to help students practise applied research skills and build material that can inform their final group research brief/report. Students are encouraged to use feedback from tutors, peers and workshop activities to revise and strengthen their work across the semester.

Because students may choose which five weekly assessments to submit, students should plan ahead and consider how the selected tasks will support their contribution to the group project.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Online submission via Blackboard only by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission details. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
19/10/2026 - 26/10/2026

During students' timetabled workshop time in Week 12 or Week 13.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment involves a concise three-minute applied research pitch based on the group research project. This task is modelled on a Three Minute Thesis-style format, but adapted for applied environmental research. Groups will deliver their pitches during class in Weeks 12 and 13. Groups will be able to select their preferred week (depending on availability).

The purpose of the pitch is to communicate the project clearly, persuasively and succinctly to a non-specialist applied audience. Groups should explain the environmental issue or problem they investigated, their research question, the method or approach used, the key insight or finding, and the significance of the research for environmental management, planning, policy or practice.

Each group will have a strict three-minute time limit and may use one static slide. The pitch should not attempt to summarise every detail of the research report. Instead, it should present the core logic and value of the project: what the issue is, what the group investigated, how they approached it, what matters most, and why the research is useful.

Marks will be awarded for clarity of the research problem and question, appropriateness of the method explanation, strength of the key message or insight, applied relevance, and quality of oral and visual communication. Assessment will be recorded.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: No late submission is allowed. Students must present at their scheduled time.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The aim of this assessment is to allow students to create and deliver a research project from start to finish. Throughout the semester, students will be working in small groups on a research topic/issue. Groups are invited to choose a topic that they are interested in. The group will:

1.     Identify and provide an overview of the research topic,

2.     Design an overarching research question and a series of aims,

3.     Provide a brief overview of relevant literature,

4.     Design and justify a method for data collection and analysis including key limitations,

5.     Distil the key results/findings,

6.     Provide a discussion of the findings in relation to the literature in this area, and

7.     Offer recommendations and future research directions.

More details about this assessment, including Marking Criteria, is provided in the ‘Assessments’ Folder on the course Blackboard site.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Online submission by Turnitin only by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission link. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
The aim of this assessment is to help students reflect on their learning across the course, their development as applied researchers, their contribution to the group project, and their engagement with peer feedback and revision.

Students should use the reflective workbook to explain what they have learned through the process of developing, revising and communicating the group applied research project. The workbook should include reflection on the following areas:

·       Individual learning and development across research design, literature, methods, data analysis, feedback, project management, communication and research ethics.

·       Own contribution to the group project, including role, decisions, challenges, collaboration and project management.

·       Feedback received and changes made to the project over time.

·       The peer feedback process, including what the student learned from reviewing others’ work and how giving feedback helped them think differently about their own project.

·       Evidence of providing constructive feedback on other students’ draft work, such as completed peer review templates, annotated comments, feedback notes or equivalent records.

·       Responsible use of AI and/or machine translation tools, where relevant.

The workbook must include evidence of the student’s engagement in the peer feedback process. This includes evidence that the student provided constructive feedback on other students’ or groups’ draft work. The reflection should also discuss what made the feedback useful or difficult to act on, and how peer feedback informed subsequent revisions to the group research brief/report.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT)

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
